title: When Has My Intuition Been Right?
subtitle: A journal practice for building an evidence base for your own inner knowing
excerpt: A journal practice for assembling the record of times your intuition was accurate. For rebuilding trust in your own inner sense after a pattern of talking yourself out of it.

practice.whenToUse: Use when you are trying to rebuild trust in your intuition, or when you need a factual account of your own inner accuracy before making a decision you are unsure about.

content.text: A journal practice for building an evidence base for your own inner knowing. Distrust of intuition is often general: the broad sense that gut feelings are unreliable, emotional, or irrational. The evidence is typically more specific than the distrust: instances where the knowing was right, overridden, and then vindicated. This practice collects that evidence.
List three times your gut was right and you knew it before the evidence arrived.
Relationships, decisions, situations, people. Times you sensed something and it turned out to be accurate. Write the facts, not the meaning.
What did the knowing feel like in your body before you acted on it (or didn't)?
Location, quality, timing. This is your intuition's signature. Learning to recognise it is the first step in trusting it.
List two or three times your intuition was wrong or led you somewhere unhelpful.
Include these. A fair account means both. Notice whether those instances have anything in common: emotional state, urgency, wishful thinking.
What conditions make your intuition most reliable for you?
Calm, sleep, distance from the situation, early in the morning. Write the conditions your clearest inner knowing arrives in.
